raw_case:
A large thyroglossal duct cyst with atypical features in a 73-year-old male: A case report


Introduction

Thyroglossal duct cysts (TGDCs) are congenital anomalies arising from incomplete obliteration of the thyroglossal duct (TGD), typically presenting in childhood. While rare in the elderly, they can occur in adulthood and may remain asymptomatic until complications such as infection or malignant transformation arise.

Presentation of case

A 73-year-old male presented with progressively enlarging left-sided neck swelling over 15 years. Clinical and radiological evaluation confirmed the diagnosis of a TGDC. Surgical excision was performed via the Sistrunk procedure, with post-operative histopathology confirming the pre-operative diagnosis of TGDC. The patient recovered well with no complications or recurrence.

Discussion

Clinical presentation of TGDC is typically an anterior neck enlargement or a non-tender midline neck mass, moving with swallowing, related to the hyoid bone. The typical differential diagnosis of TGDCs include cystic and midline neck masses. Atypically, our patient had a lateral cystic mass which was immobile with swallowing.

Conclusion

This case highlights the importance of considering TGDCs as a differential diagnosis in elderly patients despite their rarity. TGDCs may present with complications or atypical features of TGDCs which require more evaluation. Surgical intervention, specifically the Sistrunk procedure, remains the optimal management strategy for TGDCs, offering excellent outcomes and reducing the risk of recurrence.

Highlights

•TGDCs can occur in the elderly, expanding our understanding of this condition beyond its usual pediatric presentation.•Sistrunk procedure remains an effective and safe treatment for TGDCs, even in older patients.•Atypical presentation as a lateral mass and swallowing immobility is noted in patients with TGDC.1

Introduction

Thyroglossal duct cysts (TGDCs) are the most common congenital neck masses, arising from the persistent remnants of the thyroglossal duct (TGD) during embryonic development [1,2]. While typically presenting in childhood, TGDCs can also occur in adulthood, albeit less frequently, with cases in the elderly being exceptionally rare [1]. These cysts are characterized by a midline neck mass, often located near the hyoid bone, which moves with swallowing [1,3]. Most TGDCs are asymptomatic, but they can become infected, leading to abscess formation [3]. Malignant transformation of TGDCs is rare, occurring in less than 1 % of cases [3]. This report presents an unusual case of a large TGDC in an elderly patient.2

Presentation of case

A diabetic 73-year-old male with hypertension and a previous history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) presented with a large neck swelling more pronounced at the left side of the neck, alongside with dysphagia, ear pain and feeling of discomfort in the pharynx in the last few months. Clinical examination showed a non-tender cystic swelling unmoving with swallowing, no other manifestations were observed (Fig. 1). This swelling developed gradually over the past 15 years. The patient reported inconsistent adherence to his prescribed medications. Blood tests of the patient were within normal limits. Computed tomography (CT) imaging revealed a large and well-defined thin-wall unilocular round cystic mass on the left side of the neck, which caused a deviation of the trachea and other components to the right, with no invasion of the surrounding tissues (Fig. 2). Clinical and radiological features matched the diagnosis of a TGDC. A FNA was performed but the histopathological report showed only benign cytology findings of inflammatory type (Fig. 3). A surgical excision was performed, removing the cyst, the middle third of the hyoid bone, and the tract (Sistrunk operation), The atypical presentation in our case, characterized by lateralization and immobility with swallowing, is likely attributable to the presence of extensive fibrosis surrounding the thyroglossal duct cyst. This fibrosis, a consequence of the cyst's prolonged presence, may have impeded the cyst's normal mobility during swallowing and contributed to its gradual displacement toward the lateral aspect of the neck. The total resected specimen was sent for histopathological evaluation. The Histopathological report of the sample confirmed the diagnosis of TGDC, showing a cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, underlined with basal membrane and thin fibrous tissue with benign thyroid follicles with no malignancy (Fig. 4). The patient's wound exhibited complete healing without any complications. Subsequent follow-up assessments for a year indicated no evidence of recurrence.3

Discussion

Neck swellings typically arise from lymph nodes, thyroid, salivary glands and soft tissues [4,5]. TGDC is known to be the most prevalent among pediatric masses, mostly occurring as congenital anomalies but can also be observed at varying frequencies in adults, it can form anywhere on the migration route of the thyroid [3]. Our case discusses an unusual presentation of a large TGDC in an elderly patient who is 73 years old. This is unusual for this mass to appear in such ages especially when the patient reported that this swelling was gradually developed asymptomatically over the past 15 years [3]. While the classical presentation of a TGDC involves an anterior, midline neck swelling, mobile with swallowing and tethered to the hyoid bone [7], our case deviated from this typical pattern. The patient presented with a lateral cystic mass that remained immobile during swallowing, a finding likely attributable to extensive fibrosis surrounding the lesion. The differential diagnosis of lateral cervical masses is broad and encompasses a range of benign and malignant entities. These include, but are not limited to, hemangioma, branchial cyst, neoplastic changes in lymph nodes (metastasis and lymphoma), infective lymphadenitis, foreign body reaction, sialadenitis, neoplastic changes in salivary glands, and carotid aneurysm or carotid body tumor [6]. While clinical examination can often suggest a TGDC, definitive diagnosis and assessment of the mass extent necessitate imaging studies prior to surgical intervention [7]. This case serves to illustrate the variability in clinical presentation of TGDCs and underscores the importance of comprehensive diagnostic evaluation in cases of lateral neck masses. Ultrasound is commonly used to diagnose TGDC and CT imaging might be performed to evaluate TGDC and thyroid abnormalities [3]. TGDC appears on CT as a low-density thin-wall lesion routinely unilocular, on the migration route of the thyroid, often seen in midline related to the hyoid bone [7]. We have used CT to assess the diagnosis of TGDCs. Fine needle aspiration (FNA) is a quick and inexpensive, yet sensitive and accurate technique for neck masses evaluation [6]. we performed a FNA, but the cytology findings of the FNA was limited to inflammatory type cytology in our case. The Sistrunk surgical operation is the ideal management of TGDC which requires an extensive resection that includes a part of the tongue tissue and the middle third of the hyoid bone which reduces the recurrence rates whereas the recurrence rates reached (45 % to 55 %) in simple excision [3]. Surgical removal of the TGDC was performed in our patient to ensure the prevention of recurrent infections and the minimal risk of malignant development. There are few cases of TGDC in elderly people reported in literature including a case of an 85-year old male with a large long-standing TGDC, a case of a 65-year old male with a midline multilobular TGDC which had been growing over a year, and a case of 91-year old male with a large mass on the left side of the neck and mobile with swallowing [1,2,8].4

Conclusion

While the incidence of TGDC may be less frequent in geriatric patients, the Sistrunk procedure remains the gold standard surgical intervention for the majority of TGDC cases. Patients may present with serious complications including infection and malignant transformation. TGDCs may atypically present as a lateral mass and immobility with swallowing.

Abbreviations


TGDCsThyroglossal duct cystsTGDThyroglossal ductCOPDChronic obstructive pulmonary diseaseCTComputed tomographyFNAFine needle aspiration


Declaration of generative AI in scientific writing

None.

Methods

The Work has been reported in line with SCARE criteria [9].

Ethical approval

Not applicable because this paper has been reported from Hama University College of Human Medicine, Hama, Syria and our mentioned institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases.

Funding

None.

Author contribution

Ahed Assaf, Mousa Barboura, Saja Karaja, Suleiman Khaddour, and Nour Maoud: Data Curation, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.Nazem Dadah: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

Guarantor

Saja Karaja.

Research registration number

Not applicable.

Conflict of interest statement

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 73-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Large neck swelling, more pronounced on the left side.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive left-sided neck swelling over 15 years, with additional symptoms of dysphagia, ear pain, and discomfort in the pharynx in recent months.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
- **Personal History:** Inconsistent adherence to prescribed medications.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Non-tender cystic swelling, immobile with swallowing, located on the left side of the neck.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood tests: Normal.
  - CT imaging: Large, well-defined, thin-walled unilocular cystic mass on the left side of the neck, causing deviation of the trachea without invasiveness.
  - Fine needle aspiration (FNA): Benign inflammatory cytology findings.
  - Histopathology: Cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, supported by a basement membrane and benign thyroid follicles. No malignancy detected.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial differential diagnoses** included common causes of lateral neck masses: thyroglossal duct cyst (TGDC), branchial cleft cyst, lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant), sialadenitis, and carotid body tumor. 
- TGDC was suspected despite the patient’s atypical lateral presentation and absence of mobility with swallowing. The longstanding, gradual progression and imaging features aligned with TGDC.
- Branchial cleft cyst was less likely due to lack of infection history or sinus formation.
- Lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant) was excluded due to the benign cytology and absence of lymph node characteristics on imaging.
- Sialadenitis was considered unlikely, as imaging did not reveal involvement of salivary glands.
- Carotid body tumor and aneurysm were excluded based on imaging findings that confirmed a thin-walled, unilocular cystic mass.
- The final diagnosis was solidified following surgical excision and histopathological evaluation, confirming TGDC.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Thyroglossal duct cyst (TGDC) with atypical lateral presentation due to extensive fibrosis and longstanding progression.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 73-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Large neck swelling, more pronounced on the left side.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive left-sided neck swelling over 15 years, with additional symptoms of dysphagia, ear pain, and discomfort in the pharynx in recent months.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
- **Personal History:** Inconsistent adherence to prescribed medications.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Non-tender cystic swelling, immobile with swallowing, located on the left side of the neck.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood tests: Normal.
  - CT imaging: Large, well-defined, thin-walled unilocular cystic mass on the left side of the neck, causing deviation of the trachea without invasiveness.
  - Fine needle aspiration (FNA): Benign inflammatory cytology findings.
  - Histopathology: Cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, supported by a basement membrane and benign thyroid follicles. No malignancy detected.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaint: a large neck swelling on the left side that's been progressive over 15 years. The symptoms of dysphagia, ear pain, and pharyngeal discomfort are recent. The physical exam shows a non-tender, cystic, immobile mass. The CT shows a well-defined, thin-walled, unilocular cystic mass without invasiveness, and the FNA and histopathology are benign.

First, considering the long duration (15 years) and the slow progression, it's less likely to be a malignant tumor. The CT findings of a well-defined, thin-walled cyst suggest a benign process. The FNA showed benign inflammatory cells, which supports that. The histopathology is key here: ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells with a basement membrane and benign thyroid follicles. That makes me think of a thyroglossal duct cyst. Wait, but thyroglossal duct cysts are usually midline, right? But this is on the left side. Hmm.

Alternatively, a branchial cleft cyst? Those are usually on the lateral side of the neck, more common on the left. They can present as cystic masses and are often diagnosed in adults. The histology of a branchial cleft cyst typically shows stratified squamous epithelium with lymphoid tissue. But the patient's histopathology mentions ciliated pseudostratified cells, which is more like a respiratory tract lining. Wait, maybe it's a cystic lesion from the pharyngeal pouch?

Wait, the histopathology mentions ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells and benign thyroid follicles. That combination is a bit confusing. Thyroid follicles would suggest a thyroid origin. But the location is the neck, not the thyroid gland itself. Could this be a thyroglossal duct cyst with some thyroid tissue? But thyroglossal duct cysts are lined by thyroid tissue, not ciliated cells. Alternatively, maybe a cystic lesion in the neck with both thyroid and respiratory epithelium? That seems unusual.

Another possibility is a dermoid or epidermoid cyst, but those are lined by stratified squamous epithelium, not ciliated. The presence of ciliated pseudostratified cells suggests a connection to the respiratory tract, which might point towards a branchial cleft cyst. However, branchial cleft cysts typically have squamous epithelium with lymphoid tissue. Wait, the histopathology here says "ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells" which is more like the lining of the trachea or bronchi. So maybe a bronchogenic cyst? But bronchogenic cysts are usually in the mediastinum, not the neck.

Alternatively, could this be a thyroglossal duct cyst that has undergone some metaplasia? But I'm not sure. The presence of benign thyroid follicles suggests some thyroid tissue is present. Wait, the location is the left neck. If the mass is in the neck and has thyroid follicles, maybe it's a thyroid cyst, but the CT shows it's a separate mass causing tracheal deviation. Thyroid cysts are usually within the thyroid gland, not in the lateral neck. 

Wait, the patient has a history of diabetes, hypertension, and COPD, but those might not be directly related. The key is the histology. The ciliated pseudostratified cells are typical of respiratory epithelium. So maybe the mass is a branchial cleft cyst that has some ciliated lining. But branchial cleft cysts are usually lined by stratified squamous epithelium with lymphoid aggregates. However, in some cases, they might have other types of epithelium. Alternatively, maybe the histology is misinterpreted. Wait, the ciliated pseudostratified cells could be from the trachea, but how would that be in the neck? 

Wait, another thought: a cystic lesion in the neck with ciliated cells could be a bronchogenic cyst, but again, those are more mediastinal. Alternatively, a cystic teratoma? But that's less likely in a 73-year-old with no history of such. 

Alternatively, the mass could be a lymphatic malformation, but that's usually not lined by ciliated cells. 

Wait, the histopathology says "cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, supported by a basement membrane and benign thyroid follicles." So, the wall has both ciliated cells and thyroid follicles. That's unusual. Maybe it's a thyroglossal duct cyst with some ciliated metaplasia? Or a mixed lesion? 

Alternatively, could this be a cystic lesion of the thyroid gland that's displaced? But the CT shows it's causing tracheal deviation, which suggests it's a separate mass. 

Wait, the presence of thyroid follicles in the cyst wall might indicate that the cyst is derived from the thyroid, but located in the neck. Thyroglossal duct cysts are remnants of the thyroglossal duct and are usually midline. However, they can sometimes be off-midline. If the patient had a thyroglossal duct cyst that's located on the left side, that could be possible. The ciliated cells might be an error in the histopathology report, or maybe the cells are from a different origin. 

Alternatively, could this be a cystic lesion of the pharynx or larynx that's extending into the neck? But the physical exam says the mass is on the left neck, immobile with swallowing. 

Another angle: the FNA showed benign inflammatory cells, which is consistent with a benign cyst. The CT shows no invasiveness, which is reassuring. The histopathology's mention of thyroid follicles might be a clue. If the mass is a thyroglossal duct cyst, but the ciliated cells are a misinterpretation. Or perhaps the ciliated cells are from the trachea, and the thyroid follicles are a separate finding. 

Wait, maybe the mass is a combination of a thyroglossal duct cyst and a bronchogenic cyst? That seems unlikely. 

Alternatively, the histopathology might have a mix of tissues. If the cyst wall has both ciliated cells and thyroid follicles, it might be a rare variant. But I'm not sure. 

Given the information, the most likely diagnosis is a branchial cleft cyst, but the histology doesn't fit perfectly. Alternatively, a thyroglossal duct cyst with some unusual features. However, since the histology mentions thyroid follicles, that might lean towards a thyroglossal duct cyst. But the ciliated cells are confusing. 

Wait, maybe the ciliated cells are from the trachea, and the mass is a tracheal cyst. But tracheal cysts are rare and usually in the mediastinum. 

Alternatively, the patient might have a thyroglossal duct cyst that's located on the left side, and the ciliated cells are a misinterpretation. Or perhaps the histopathology is correct, and the diagnosis is a rare variant. 

Given the presence of thyroid follicles, the most plausible diagnosis is a thyroglossal duct cyst, even though it's on the left side. The ciliated cells might be an error or a misinterpretation. Alternatively, the mass could be a dermoid cyst with some thyroid tissue, but that's less likely. 

Considering all the factors, the best fit is a thyroglossal duct cyst, despite the location. Alternatively, a branchial cleft cyst. But the histology with thyroid follicles points more towards thyroglossal duct. So, I think the final diagnosis is thyroglossal duct cyst.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a long-standing (15-year) progressive left-sided neck swelling, which is non-tender, cystic, and immobile on swallowing. This chronic, non-inflammatory presentation suggests a benign process rather than an acute infection or malignancy. The absence of tenderness and the lack of systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss) further support a benign etiology.

<step 2> Imaging (CT) reveals a well-defined, thin-walled, unilocular cystic mass without invasiveness, which is consistent with a benign cystic lesion. The deviation of the trachea indicates the size and location of the mass but does not suggest malignancy, as there is no evidence of infiltration or metastasis.

<step 3> Fine needle aspiration (FNA) shows benign inflammatory cytology, which aligns with a non-malignant process. However, FNA is not definitive for histopathological diagnosis, necessitating further histological evaluation.

<step 4> Histopathology is critical here: the cyst wall is lined by **ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells** (suggestive of respiratory tract origin) and contains **benign thyroid follicles**. This combination is highly characteristic of a **thyroglossal duct cyst**. While thyroglossal duct cysts are typically midline, they can occasionally be off-midline due to embryological anomalies or displacement. The presence of thyroid follicles confirms a thyroid-derived origin, and the ciliated cells may represent metaplastic changes or artifacts from the cystic environment.

<step 5> Alternative diagnoses such as branchial cleft cysts (usually lined by stratified squamous epithelium with lymphoid tissue) or bronchogenic cysts (typically mediastinal) are less likely given the histopathology and location. The absence of malignancy in histopathology rules out neoplastic processes.

### Answer:
Thyroglossal
